Mapping a City’s Road Network with OpenStreetMap

Analysis
Python
OSM
Basic
Published

30/03/2026

Overview

The goal of this project is to extract, clean, and visualize the road network of Hod Hasharon using OpenStreetMap data, while demonstrating a basic spatial workflow and basic Python tools like OSMnx, geopandas and matplotlib.

Hod Hasharon is a typical suburban city, with population of over 65,000. Most of the population works outside the city, which creates heavy traffic on the outer axis routes, especially during morning hours.

Spatial tools used in this project

GeoPandas for a spatial analysis.

OSMnx is a Python package to easily download, model, analyze, and visualize street networks and other geospatial features from OpenStreetMap.

Work stages

This basic workflow include:

  • Download spatial data from OpenStreetMap using OSMnx
  • Basic GeoDataFrame inspection and cleaning
  • CRS transformation and why it matters
  • Simple spatial analysis (length by road type)
  • Basic static and interactive visualization

Getting the data

We’ll start by importing the necessary packages:

Code 
import osmnx as ox
import pandas as pd
import geopandas as gpd

Define the place / boundaries

First, we’ll get the city boundaries using the ox.geocode_to_gdf() function. This has 2 main roles:

  • Make sure that OSM recognizes the place’s name.

  • Create a polygon of the place’s border which we can use later

Code 
city_name = "Hod Hasharon, Israel"

boundary = ox.geocode_to_gdf(city_name)
boundary.head()
geometry bbox_west bbox_south bbox_east bbox_north place_id osm_type osm_id lat lon class type place_rank importance addresstype name display_name
0 POLYGON ((34.87115 32.13514, 34.87122 32.13287... 34.871145 32.128648 34.925727 32.171671 194746259 way 90998117 32.14995 34.885125 place town 18 0.535236 town Hod HaSharon Hod HaSharon, Petah Tikva Subdistrict, Center ...

boundary object type: geopandas.geodataframe.GeoDataFrame

boundary CRS:

Code 
# Confirm CRS:
boundary.crs
<Geographic 2D CRS: EPSG:4326>
Name: WGS 84
Axis Info [ellipsoidal]:
- Lat[north]: Geodetic latitude (degree)
- Lon[east]: Geodetic longitude (degree)
Area of Use:
- name: World.
- bounds: (-180.0, -90.0, 180.0, 90.0)
Datum: World Geodetic System 1984 ensemble
- Ellipsoid: WGS 84
- Prime Meridian: Greenwich

boundary Basic plot:

Code 
# Basic plot
boundary.plot()

Load the roads dataset

Note

In this section we’ll load the full dataset and clean the data after loading, using geopandas

Now we can load the roads and paths. First we need to define the tags.
tags is the key:value of the map feature we want to load. full list of the tags can be found here.
We’ll use 'road_type': True to retrieve the whole roads, paths and cycle tracks and other recognised routes.

OSMnx has a few features_from_*() methods to load data, in which we’ll focus on 2 of them.

features_from_place()

This method uses the place name we confirmed earlier, it’s simpler method but depends on OSM’s place definitions:

Code 
# Defining 'tags' (the value can be a list of specific features)
tags = {'highway': True}

# Using features_from_place()
city_roads = ox.features_from_place(city_name, tags=tags)

We got 2008 features with 85 attributes

features_from_polygon()

In this method we use the polygon object we created, which gives more control and especially useful with custom boundaries:

Code 
# Using features_from_polygon()
city_roads_from_polygon = ox.features_from_polygon(boundary.geometry.iloc[0],
                                           tags=tags)
Note

The polygon argument is boundary.geometry.iloc[0] which is shapely.geometry.polygon.Polygon type object

Here we also got 2008 features with 85 attributes

Cleaning and filtering

Code 
city_roads.head()
geometry highway traffic_signals crossing crossing:island crossing:markings bus gtfs:stop_code:IL-MOT gtfs:stop_id:IL-MOT gtfs:stop_name:IL-MOT ... oneway:mtb handrail incline ramp cycleway cycleway:surface footway:surface note tunnel type
element id
node 370252672 POINT (34.89455 32.15582) traffic_signals NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN ... NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN
370252702 POINT (34.89426 32.1592) traffic_signals NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN ... NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN
387220797 POINT (34.89405 32.16281) traffic_signals NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN ... NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN
555149737 POINT (34.90121 32.16815) traffic_signals signal NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN ... NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN
599906809 POINT (34.89981 32.13304) traffic_signals NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN ... NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN

5 rows × 85 columns

The 2 main things we can see from the start:

  1. Not all the features are LineStrings, some of them are points (like bus stops traffic signals and so on)

  2. There are 85 attributes which most are NaN

Filtering geometry types

Let’s check the geometry type we got, and filter out all non-LineString features

Code 
city_roads.geometry.geom_type.value_counts()
LineString    1776
Point          227
Polygon          5
Name: count, dtype: int64
Code 
city_roads_linestrings = city_roads[
    city_roads.geometry.geom_type.isin(["LineString", "MultiLineString"])]
Tip

Although there aren’t any MultiLineString geometric types features in this gdf, it’s a good practice to include them.

After filtering, the gdf contains 1776 features

Cleaning columns

The necessary columns are:

  • ‘geometry’ - IMPORTANT!

  • ‘highway’ - The road type according to the OSM’s key:value definition (will be changed to road_type)

  • ‘name’ - Streets name (if available)

Code 
city_roads_linestrings = city_roads_linestrings[['geometry', 'highway', 'name']]
city_roads_linestrings.rename({"highway": "road_type"}, axis="columns", inplace=True) # Rename column

The clean gdf contains 1776 features and 3 attributes

Analysis

Now it’s time for a simple analysis. Let’s find out the 'road_type', their total length and their percentage from the total roads’ length.

Reproject the CRS

Before we add the length of the segments, we have to check the gdf’s CRS to see if it has meaningful units of measurement:

Code 
# Check gdf CRS 
city_roads_linestrings.crs
<Geographic 2D CRS: EPSG:4326>
Name: WGS 84
Axis Info [ellipsoidal]:
- Lat[north]: Geodetic latitude (degree)
- Lon[east]: Geodetic longitude (degree)
Area of Use:
- name: World.
- bounds: (-180.0, -90.0, 180.0, 90.0)
Datum: World Geodetic System 1984 ensemble
- Ellipsoid: WGS 84
- Prime Meridian: Greenwich
Code 
city_roads_linestrings.geometry.length.head()
element  id      
way      31661888    0.008288
         31664220    0.004763
         31664319    0.001693
         31664320    0.001673
         31664581    0.003211
dtype: float64

In WGS84 (EPSG: 4326) coordinates and measurements are in degrees, which make distance values hard to understand and not very practical on small areas. To make measurements meaningful we need to reproject the spatial dataset to a projected CRS (ex. UTM) where units are in meters.

Tip

For a full list of all the available CRSs, check epsg.io site

Code 
city_roads_UTM = city_roads_linestrings.to_crs(epsg=32636)

So now, if we check the CRS we’ll get:

Code 
city_roads_UTM.crs
<Projected CRS: EPSG:32636>
Name: WGS 84 / UTM zone 36N
Axis Info [cartesian]:
- E[east]: Easting (metre)
- N[north]: Northing (metre)
Area of Use:
- name: Between 30°E and 36°E, northern hemisphere between equator and 84°N, onshore and offshore. Belarus. Cyprus. Egypt. Ethiopia. Finland. Israel. Jordan. Kenya. Lebanon. Moldova. Norway. Russian Federation. Saudi Arabia. Sudan. Syria. Türkiye (Turkey). Uganda. Ukraine.
- bounds: (30.0, 0.0, 36.0, 84.0)
Coordinate Operation:
- name: UTM zone 36N
- method: Transverse Mercator
Datum: World Geodetic System 1984 ensemble
- Ellipsoid: WGS 84
- Prime Meridian: Greenwich

And if we check the length of the first few features, we’ll get:

Code 
city_roads_UTM.geometry.length.head()
element  id      
way      31661888    781.878730
         31664220    528.026825
         31664319    173.824444
         31664320    168.902683
         31664581    355.949285
dtype: float64

Adding ‘length (m)’ attribute

Code 
city_roads_UTM['length (m)'] = round(city_roads_UTM.geometry.length, 2)
city_roads_UTM.head()
geometry road_type name length (m)
element id
way 31661888 LINESTRING (677111.155 3559979.322, 677120.526... tertiary משה שרת 781.88
31664220 LINESTRING (677908.092 3560027.002, 677907.53 ... tertiary בית העם 528.03
31664319 LINESTRING (677624.453 3560007.537, 677511.922... living_street ארלוזורוב 173.82
31664320 LINESTRING (677638.008 3559750.042, 677511.778... living_street רמז 168.90
31664581 LINESTRING (677334.865 3559994.22, 677332.286 ... residential הפרדס 355.95

Calculating the total length of each ‘road_type’ value

Code 
roads_types = pd.DataFrame(city_roads_UTM.groupby('road_type')['length (m)'].sum())
roads_types['percent of total length'] = round((roads_types['length (m)'] / sum(roads_types['length (m)']) * 100), 2)
roads_types.sort_values(by='length (m)', ascending=False)
length (m) percent of total length
road_type
residential 92854.33 39.59
path 26805.91 11.43
tertiary 23675.85 10.10
track 20928.70 8.92
footway 20515.27 8.75
service 17467.23 7.45
cycleway 10840.30 4.62
primary 9515.90 4.06
living_street 6876.08 2.93
secondary 3902.65 1.66
primary_link 453.12 0.19
motorway_link 314.84 0.13
steps 204.12 0.09
secondary_link 128.78 0.05
pedestrian 29.67 0.01

Plotting

Before we plot the streets, we’ll define an ordered categorical type of the highway values according to OSM and apply it to the 'road_type' column in the gdf

Code 
# Create ordered list of highway values in the gdf
from pandas.api.types import CategoricalDtype

highway_types_ordered = CategoricalDtype(categories=["primary", "secondary", "tertiary", "residential", "motorway_link", "primary_link", "secondary_link", "living_street", "service", "pedestrian", "track", "footway", "steps", "path", "cycleway"])

# Attaching the order to the gdf
city_roads_UTM['road_type'] = city_roads_UTM['road_type'].astype(highway_types_ordered)

Now we can create a simple plot of Hod Hasharon’s streets with the categorical symbology:

Code 
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 8))
city_roads_UTM.plot(ax=ax,
                    column='road_type', 
                    cmap="plasma_r", 
                    legend=True,
                    categorical=True,
                    legend_kwds={"loc": "lower right",
                                 "ncol": 2,
                                 "title": "OSM Road Types",
                                 "fontsize": 9})
ax.set_axis_off()
plt.title("Hod Hasharon - Street Types (OpenStreetMap)", size=20)

plt.show()
Code 
city_roads_UTM.explore(column='road_type',
                       cmap="plasma_r",
                       tiles='CartoDB positron'
                       )
Make this Notebook Trusted to load map: File -> Trust Notebook

Interactive map

Conclusion

As expected, in a suburban city like Hod Hasharon, most of the streets are residential type (~40%), in contrast to the small amount of primary and secondary types of roads (~6%) which play a key role in outer connectivity. This may be a contributing factor to morning traffic congestion.
The relatively high share of paths, footways and cycleways (~25%) also suggests a mixed-use urban environment with accessible pedestrian and cycling routes.

Summary

In this project we used a basic workflow to:

  • Extract roads data from OpenStreetMap
  • Clean and filter geometries
  • Reproject data for accurate measurements
  • Calculate road lengths by type
  • Visualize the results

This workflow can serve as a foundation for more advanced spatial analysis projects.

Advanced action-items

Here’s some advanced action-items which can be made on this project:

  • Divide the road types into 3 bins - connectivity roads, local roads, walking pathways and bicycle routes
  • Compare with other cities
  • Improve visualization
